data.content:
### Configure Project Dependencies for Global Setup in Playwright

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/test-api/class-testproject.md

Shows how to configure project dependencies to ensure setup tasks run before test execution. The setup project runs global.setup.ts first, followed by browser-specific projects (chromium, firefox, webkit) that depend on it. Dependencies enable setup actions to produce traces, artifacts, and appear in test reports while maintaining test-like structure.

```javascript
import { defineConfig } from '@playwright/test';

export default defineConfig({
  projects: [
    {
      name: 'setup',
      testMatch: /global.setup\.ts/,
    },
    {
      name: 'chromium',
      use: devices['Desktop Chrome'],
      dependencies: ['setup'],
    },
    {
      name: 'firefox',
      use: devices['Desktop Firefox'],
      dependencies: ['setup'],
    },
    {
      name: 'webkit',
      use: devices['Desktop Safari'],
      dependencies: ['setup'],
    },
  ],
});
```

--------------------------------

### Define Playwright Test Project Dependencies for Setup (JavaScript/TypeScript)

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/test-projects-js.md

This configuration illustrates how to set up project dependencies in Playwright. A 'setup' project runs first, and then 'chromium', 'firefox', and 'webkit' projects depend on it, ensuring setup actions complete before browser-specific tests begin. This allows for global setup actions like logging in or database setup.

```js
import { defineConfig, devices } from '@playwright/test';

export default defineConfig({
  projects: [
    {
      name: 'setup',
      testMatch: '**/*.setup.ts',
    },
    {
      name: 'chromium',
      use: { ...devices['Desktop Chrome'] },
      dependencies: ['setup'],
    },
    {
      name: 'firefox',
      use: { ...devices['Desktop Firefox'] },
      dependencies: ['setup'],
    },
    {
      name: 'webkit',
      use: { ...devices['Desktop Safari'] },
      dependencies: ['setup'],
    },
  ],
});
```

--------------------------------

### Install Dependencies with npm

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/tests/components/ct-vue-vite/README.md

Installs all project dependencies defined in package.json for the Vue 3 Vite project. This is the first step in project setup and must be executed before running development or production builds.

```shell
npm install
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Playwright project dependencies (TypeScript)

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/release-notes-js.md

This TypeScript configuration demonstrates how to define dependencies between Playwright projects using the `TestProject.dependencies` property. This feature enables global setup to produce traces and artifacts, enhancing test reporting and setup visibility.

```js
import { defineConfig, devices } from '@playwright/test';

export default defineConfig({
    projects: [
      {
        name: 'setup',
        testMatch: /global.setup\.ts/,
      },
      {
        name: 'chromium',
        use: devices['Desktop Chrome'],
        dependencies: ['setup'],
      },
      {
        name: 'firefox',
        use: devices['Desktop Firefox'],
        dependencies: ['setup'],
      },
      {
        name: 'webkit',
        use: devices['Desktop Safari'],
        dependencies: ['setup'],
      },
    ],
  });
```

--------------------------------

### Install Dependencies and Start Development Server

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/tests/components/ct-svelte/README.md

After creating a new Svelte project, install the necessary dependencies using npm and then start the development server with Rollup. The app will be accessible at localhost:8080.

```bash
cd svelte-app
npm install
npm run dev
```

--------------------------------

### Install Dependencies and Build Playwright (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md

Installs project dependencies using `npm ci` for clean installation, starts the build process in watch mode for continuous compilation, and then installs necessary Playwright browsers. This prepares the development environment for making changes.

```bash
npm ci
npm run watch
npx playwright install
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Project Dependencies for Global Setup in Playwright Config

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/test-global-setup-teardown-js.md

Defines a setup project named 'setup db' that matches the global.setup.ts file and configures another project to depend on it. This approach allows global setup to run before other tests while producing traces and artifacts that appear in HTML reports.

```javascript
import { defineConfig, devices } from '@playwright/test';

export default defineConfig({
  testDir: './tests',
  // ...
  projects: [
    {
      name: 'setup db',
      testMatch: /global\.setup\.ts/,
    },
    {
      name: 'chromium with db',
      use: { ...devices['Desktop Chrome'] },
      dependencies: ['setup db'],
    },
  ]
});
```

--------------------------------

### Add Playwright Dependency to Maven (pom.xml)

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/intro-java.md

This Maven `pom.xml` configuration snippet adds the Playwright Java client library as a dependency to a project. It also configures the `maven-compiler-plugin` to ensure compatibility with Java 1.8 or higher, which is a Playwright requirement. Remember to replace `%%VERSION%%` with the appropriate Playwright release version.

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<project xmlns="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0" xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
  xsi:schemaLocation="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0 http://maven.apache.org/xsd/maven-4.0.0.xsd">
  <modelVersion>4.0.0</modelVersion>

  <groupId>org.example</groupId>
  <artifactId>examples</artifactId>
  <version>0.1-SNAPSHOT</version>
  <name>Playwright Client Examples</name>
  <properties>
    <project.build.sourceEncoding>UTF-8</project.build.sourceEncoding>
  </properties>
  <dependencies>
    <dependency>
      <groupId>com.microsoft.playwright</groupId>
      <artifactId>playwright</artifactId>
      <version>%%VERSION%%</version>
    </dependency>
  </dependencies>
  <build>
    <plugins>
      <plugin>
        <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
        <artifactId>maven-compiler-plugin</artifactId>
        <version>3.10.1</version>
        <!-- References to interface static methods are allowed only at source level 1.8 or above -->
        <configuration>
          <source>1.8</source>
          <target>1.8</target>
        </configuration>
      </plugin>
    </plugins>
  </build>
</project>
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Playwright Project Teardown with Database Cleanup - TypeScript

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/test-global-setup-teardown-js.md

Configures a Playwright test project with setup and teardown dependencies. The setup project 'setup db' runs first, followed by test execution in dependent projects like 'chromium', and finally the teardown project 'cleanup db' executes to clean up resources. This pattern ensures proper test isolation and resource management.

```typescript
import { defineConfig } from '@playwright/test';

export default defineConfig({
  testDir: './tests',
  // ...
  projects: [
    {
      name: 'setup db',
      testMatch: /global\.setup\.ts/,
      teardown: 'cleanup db',
    },
    {
      name: 'cleanup db',
      testMatch: /global\.teardown\.ts/,
    },
    {
      name: 'chromium',
      use: { ...devices['Desktop Chrome'] },
      dependencies: ['setup db'],
    },
  ]
});
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Playwright projects with teardown in JavaScript/TypeScript

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/release-notes-js.md

This configuration snippet demonstrates how to use the `teardown` property within Playwright projects. It allows specifying a project to run after a primary project and its dependencies have finished, typically used for cleaning up resources, and is often paired with a `setup` dependency.

```js
import { defineConfig } from '@playwright/test';

export default defineConfig({
  projects: [
    {
      name: 'setup',
      testMatch: /global.setup\.ts/,
      teardown: 'teardown',
    },
    {
      name: 'teardown',
      testMatch: /global.teardown\.ts/,
    },
    {
      name: 'chromium',
      use: devices['Desktop Chrome'],
      dependencies: ['setup'],
    },
    {
      name: 'firefox',
      use: devices['Desktop Firefox'],
      dependencies: ['setup'],
    },
    {
      name: 'webkit',
      use: devices['Desktop Safari'],
      dependencies: ['setup'],
    },
  ],
});
```

--------------------------------

### Install Playwright Library for Node.js Projects

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/library-js.md

This command installs the core Playwright library as a development dependency in your Node.js project. It provides the necessary APIs for browser automation tasks.

```bash
npm i -D playwright
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Playwright setup and teardown projects

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/test-api/class-testproject.md

This example shows how to define `setup` and `teardown` projects in `playwright.config.ts`. The `setup` project is executed first, and the `teardown` project runs after all dependent projects have finished, ensuring proper resource acquisition and cleanup within your test suite.

```typescript
import { defineConfig } from '@playwright/test';

export default defineConfig({
  projects: [
    {
      name: 'setup',
      testMatch: /global.setup\.ts/,
      teardown: 'teardown',
    },
    {
      name: 'teardown',
      testMatch: /global.teardown\.ts/,
    },
    {
      name: 'chromium',
      use: devices['Desktop Chrome'],
      dependencies: ['setup'],
    },
    {
      name: 'firefox',
      use: devices['Desktop Firefox'],
      dependencies: ['setup'],
    },
    {
      name: 'webkit',
      use: devices['Desktop Safari'],
      dependencies: ['setup'],
    },
  ],
});
```

--------------------------------

### Install Playwright Browsers and Dependencies

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/test-cli-js.md

Installs browsers required by Playwright for testing. It provides options to install all browsers, specific browsers like Chromium or WebKit, or their system dependencies. An `install-deps` command is available for dependencies, and `uninstall` for removal.

```bash
npx playwright install [options] [browser...]
npx playwright install-deps [options] [browser...]
npx playwright uninstall
```

```bash
# Install all browsers
npx playwright install
```

```bash
# Install only Chromium
npx playwright install chromium
```

```bash
# Install specific browsers
npx playwright install chromium webkit
```

```bash
# Install browsers with dependencies
npx playwright install --with-deps
```

--------------------------------

### Clone and Build Playwright Project

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/tests/bidi/README.md

Initial setup commands to clone the Playwright repository, install dependencies, and build the project. Includes option for watch mode during development. Run these commands sequentially in your terminal.

```shell
git clone https://github.com/microsoft/playwright.git
cd playwright
npm run build
npx playwright install chromium
npm run biditest -- --project='moz-firefox-*'
```

--------------------------------

### Install Playwright and Browsers in CI Environments

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/ci.md

This snippet provides commands to install Playwright and its necessary browser dependencies across various programming language ecosystems within a Continuous Integration (CI) pipeline. It covers installations for JavaScript, Python, Java, and C# projects, ensuring the CI agent is ready to execute tests.

```bash
# Install NPM packages
npm ci

# Install Playwright browsers and dependencies
npx playwright install --with-deps
```

```bash
pip install playwright
playwright install --with-deps
```

```bash
mvn exec:java -e -D exec.mainClass=com.microsoft.playwright.CLI -D exec.args="install --with-deps"
```

```bash
dotnet build
pwsh bin/Debug/netX/playwright.ps1 install --with-deps
```

--------------------------------

### Install NPM Dependencies for Flakiness Dashboard Backend

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/utils/flakiness-dashboard/README.md

Navigates to the `utils/flakiness-dashboard/` directory and installs Node.js package dependencies using npm. The generated `node_modules/` folder will be included when the function is published.

```bash
cd utils/flakiness-dashboard/
npm install
```

--------------------------------

### Install System Dependencies for Specific Browser via CLI

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/browsers.md

Installs system dependencies for a specific browser (e.g., Chromium) needed by Playwright. This allows for targeted installation of only the dependencies required for a particular browser, saving resources.

```js
npx playwright install-deps chromium
```

```java
mvn exec:java -e -D exec.mainClass=com.microsoft.playwright.CLI -D exec.args="install-deps chromium"
```

```python
playwright install-deps chromium
```

```csharp
pwsh bin/Debug/netX/playwright.ps1 install-deps chromium
```

--------------------------------

### Install Playwright Test via npm

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/release-notes-js.md

This command installs the Playwright Test runner as a development dependency in your project, enabling end-to-end testing features. It sets up the necessary packages to start writing and executing tests.

```bash
npm i -D @playwright/test
```

--------------------------------

### Compile and Execute Playwright Java Application (Maven CLI)

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/intro-java.md

This bash command uses Maven to compile the Java project and then execute the `main` method of the `org.example.App` class. Running this command will automatically download Playwright browser binaries and other dependencies required for execution. Ensure your `pom.xml` is correctly configured with the Playwright dependency.

```bash
mvn compile exec:java -D exec.mainClass="org.example.App"
```

--------------------------------

### Install System Dependencies for Playwright via CLI

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/browsers.md

Installs system dependencies required by Playwright using the command-line interface. This is particularly useful in CI environments to ensure all necessary libraries are present for browser execution.

```js
npx playwright install-deps
```

```java
mvn exec:java -e -D exec.mainClass=com.microsoft.playwright.CLI -D exec.args="install-deps"
```

```python
playwright install-deps
```

```csharp
pwsh bin/Debug/netX/playwright.ps1 install-deps
```

--------------------------------

### Write Test That Depends on Global Setup in Playwright

Source: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/test-global-setup-teardown-js.md

Demonstrates a test file that runs after the global setup completes. The test automatically benefits from the setup project's initialization work, such as database creation, without requiring explicit dependencies in the test code.

```javascript
import { test, expect } from '@playwright/test';

test('menu', async ({ page }) => {
  // Your test that depends on the database
});
```
